rows.vq: if we click on the dividend growth tab here on Seeking Alpha one of the things we can do is see the three five and 10year dividend growth rate and like I just mentioned this company has been increasing the amount to payout in dividends for 19 consecutive years we can see the three-year dividend growth rate sitting at 1.97% the 5year at 2% and the 10-year dividend growth rate sitting at 2.34% so yes they're growing their dividends but here's the huge problem with this if a company is not growing their dividend payments higher than the rate of inflation then that company is technically paying out less than dividends every single year so when you take into account over the past three years the economy has seen very high levels of inflation Verizon is actually technically paying out less in dividends combine this with the fact that if we jump over to the financial statements look at the statements of cash flow and again scroll down all the way to free cash flow per share it looks like Verizon's really not doing a good job of growing their free cash flow per share and all of a sudden you may be a little bit more hesitant to consider adding Verizon to your portfolio

rows.vq: Best Buy is another casualty of Amazon. The consumer electronic giant faces very stiff competition from online retailers that offer vastly more selection and better prices. And another large part of Best Buy's business was entertainment. And with much of the content available these days online only, that has also hurt Best Buy's bottom line. I remember when I was a kid, I used to love going to Best Buy and perusing the vast rows of CDs, DVDs, and video games. But that is no more at Best Buy. Now that content has shifted online, the margins tell an even bleeaker story for Best Buy. Downward trends and every important metric. This is a business suffering from its legacy brick-and-mortar retail presence.
